title: Four-shaft honeycomb weave
subtitle: A textured cell-pattern fabric on a four-shaft floor loom using a simple tie-up and treadling
excerpt: Honeycomb weave produces a fabric with raised hexagonal cells in the cloth surface: the cells trap air and give the cloth excellent insulation. The structure uses only two treadles in alternation on a four-shaft loom and is easier to treadle than twill or overshot. This entry covers a standard cotton honeycomb set up for a sampler or tea towel.
sourceNotes: Honeycomb weave is documented in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), available via Internet Archive. The structure and threading described are standard four-shaft honeycomb as presented in the Edwardian weaving tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: The raised hexagonal or rectangular pockets in honeycomb weave, created by blocks of plain weave alternating with floats in a specific threading and treadling sequence. The floats form the walls of the cell and the plain weave sections form the cell floors.

content.text: by alternating sections of plain weave and float weave in a block sequence. The floats allow the cloth to pucker and trap air in the finished fabric. On a four-shaft loom the threading uses a straight draw (shafts 1-2-3-4 repeating) with a specific treadling sequence that creates the block pattern without requiring a complex threading.

Treadle 1 and 2 alternate to produce plain weave on shafts 1+3 and 2+4 respectively. Treadles 3 and 4 produce the long floats that form the cell walls. The treadling sequence alternates a block of plain weave picks (1-2-1-2) with a block of float picks (3-4-3-4). The cell size is determined by how many picks of each block are woven before switching: more picks in each block produce taller, more pronounced cells.

items.name: 10/2 cotton weft yarn in contrast colour
Four-shaft floor loom, at least 40 cm weaving width

content.text: Set the warp at 20 ends per 10 cm (2 ends per dent in a 10-dent reed). Thread a straight draw: shaft 1, 2, 3, 4, repeat across the full width. Tie up as shown in the draft above. Begin with 4 picks on treadles 1-2 alternating (plain weave), then 4 picks on treadles 3-4 alternating (float picks). Repeat the 8-pick block throughout. Beat firmly: honeycomb needs a firm beat to set the cells clearly.

items.problem: The cells are not visible in the woven cloth
items.cause: Beat too soft or weft yarn too fine relative to the warp sett
items.fix: Firm up the beat. The float picks should be visibly looser between the plain-weave sections. Use a weft yarn at least as heavy as the warp.

items.problem: The cloth is very stiff and the cells remain flat
items.cause: Warp sett too close, preventing the float picks from raising
items.fix: Open the sett slightly (to 15 ends per 10 cm for this structure) so the floats have room to rise above the plain-weave ground.
